cip_header_fields.description: Source ID of the transmitter originating the packet
Size of one Data Block (in quadlets); a CIP can contain 0, 1 or more Data Blocks
Data Block Count, normally the sequence number for the first Data Block in the CIP
Format ID: specifies DVCR, MPEG-2, AM Protocol…
Format Dependent Field; for AM Protocol, specifies subformat (see below)
Nominal Sampling Frequency Code (part of FDF for certain AM Protocol subformats)
Time stamp (optional), using low 16 bits of IEEE1394 CYCLE_TIME register

unit_directory_entries.description: 00-01-F6 16 is the 24-bit RID (Registration Authority ID, assigned by IEEE/RAC)
01 16 is the version number that indicates the node conforms to this specification

This entry next to the Version entry specifies the offset to the MIDI-specific register space from the 64-bit private space (from FFFF FFFF E000 0000 16). This value may be changed after bus reset occurs.

description: The encapsulation function block of a receiver operating in MIDI1.0–SPEED mode shall stop receiving if it receives

transmission_modes.max_rate: up to two bytes every 320 microseconds
description: The encapsulation function block of a receiver operating in MIDI1.0–2X–SPEED mode shall stop receiving if it

transmission_modes.max_rate: up to three bytes every 320 microseconds

terminology.definition: a series of aligned quadlets conforming to a particular format. Also known as "sequence." A data sequence is normally a continuous or connected series of related events, presented in time order. However, a data sequence may also be constructed by interleaving two or more multiplexed streams of events in a prescribed manner, so as to maintain time order and relevant structural constraints.
a logical connection through which a particular data sequence or stream is transmitted. The term

Common Isochronous Packet. The basic IEEE1394 packet type used for transport of isochronous data such as video, audio and MIDI. The CIP is fully defined in IEC61883-1. A CIP overview is provided in Annex A. A partial list of CIP fields includes SID (source node of transmitter), DBS (data block size), DBC (data block count), FMT (format ID), FDF (format dependent field) and SYT (time stamp, optional).

the field within the CIP header that specifies the time when the specified event within the CIP is to be presented at a receiver. “Data Block”: a significant unit of data within a CIP (or other IEEE1394 packet). A CIP may contain 0, 1 or more Data Blocks. See discussion in Annex A.

Data Block Count, an index number for a Data Block within a particular data sequence. The CIP header has a DBC field (8 bits) which contains the Data Block Count value for the first Data Block in that CIP (just after the CIP header).
Control and Status Register defined in ISO/IEC 13213 (ANSI/IEEE 1212).

a sequence of one or more bytes that constitutes a single MIDI event. Message types include Channel Voice, Channel Mode, System Common, System Real-Time and System Exclusive, and are defined in the as follows. any number of Data bytes, and is terminated by an End of Exclusive (EOX) Status byte. The following terms are defined within “MIDI Over Alternate Media Transports”, but are not part of the “Complete MIDI 1.0 Detailed Specification”. MIDI devices, independent from the actual means of message packaging, delivery and transmission. Also known as ‘MIDI 1.0 Logical Layer’. Application-layer services include Channel, System and System Exclusive message transport; Sample Dump, File Dump, MIDI Tuning and Device Inquiry; MIDI Time Code, MIDI Show Control and MIDI Machine Control; General MIDI and Downloadable Sounds; and other services as defined in the Complete MIDI 1.0 Detailed Specification and subsequent documents. “Media Adaptation Layer”: the layer responsible for translating media data from its native format to and from the format required by a given transport layer. “MIDI 1.0 Transport Layer”: the standard media transport used for delivery of MIDI messages and MIDI streams. The MIDI 1.0 transport layer implements an isochronous unreliable simplex connection-oriented point-to-point media transport on top of the asynchronous serial MIDI 1.0 Physical Layer. layer is implemented as an asynchronous serial transmission line using an opto coupler and 5-pin DIN connector and operating at 31.25 kbps (31,250 bits per second), as defined in the Complete MIDI 1.0 Detailed Specification. The MIDI 1.0 physical layer provides isolated grounds to avoid potential audio degradation (e.g. power line hum) and safety hazards. transmission rate for such streams is limited to 31.25kbps regardless of the underlying Physical or Transport Layer.

a byte stream that consists of MIDI messages. Note: when the term "MIDI data stream" is used (with the version number "1.0" intentionally removed), it denotes a data stream which may not be limited to a transmission rate of 31.25 kbps.

an ordered collection of events that occur at the same time. When several event sequences are synchronized, it is possible to interleave these sequences into a single sequence consisting of "Clusters".
the number of sequences combined together to form a particular Cluster.
a 32-bit data element consisting of an 8-bit LABEL and a 24-bit data field. The basic data element used for encapsulating MIDI data within the proposed Audio and Music format.
denotes the format for a particular AM824 data element (8 bits).
A Channel in which one type of AM824 data sequence is being transmitted.
ordinal index of an AM824 Data Channel within a particular Cluster. The value 0 denotes the first AM824 Data Channel within the Cluster. (See [4] "AM824 Data Channel Configuration")

An AM824 Data Channel in which one or more MIDI data streams are being transmitted. The AM824 LABELs for “MIDI Conformant Data” are shown below. Only these LABELs may be used within a MIDI Conformant Data Channel:

a discontiguous sequence of AM824 elements used to carry a particular MIDI data stream. A number of MPX-MIDI Data Channels are multiplexed together within a single MIDI Conformant Data Channel. MPX-MIDI Data Channels are used only inside of the IEEE1394 media adaptation layer.

Nominal Sampling Frequency Code. This element is part of the FDF field for a CIP with FMT = "Audio and Music Data". SFC also specifies the value for SYT_INTERVAL, as shown below: Refer to "Enhancement to Audio and Music Data Transmission Protocol Version 1.0" for details.
